rule_text: At low HP on real attack turns, take the line that avoids chip damage over greedy setup or fair damage unless you can end the fight immediately.

Shiv burst decks must draft sufficient raw damage output to finish Act 3 Bosses (like Queen or Door) in 8 rounds or fewer. Winning runs cleared these late-game encounters in 6-8 rounds, while failing runs stalled into lethal attrition wars lasting 14-18 rounds due to lack of burst damage.

In Shiv archetypes, aggressively remove basic Strikes at shops and events. Winning runs consistently reduced their Strike count to 2-3 by late game, while runs that retained 4-5 Strikes suffered from dead draws, failed to generate sufficient tempo, and consistently died in Acts 2 and 3.

Prioritize upgrading Neutralize and core Shiv generators (Blade Dance, Cloak and Dagger). Winning runs heavily feature these upgrades (Neutralize+, Blade Dance+, Cloak and Dagger+) to maximize damage and mitigation per draw, while losing runs consistently left them unupgraded.

Act 1 Bosses like Lagavulin Matriarch and Waterfall Giant severely punish purely defensive stall strategies. You must secure a reliable scaling damage engine (such as poison scaling or persistent shiv generation) before Floor 17 to avoid slowly dying over 25-30 round fights.

Upgrade Neutralize early. Extending the Weak duration from 1 to 2 turns provides essential, energy-efficient damage mitigation. Most winning runs highly utilized Neutralize+, while failing runs consistently left it unupgraded or neglected it.

Prioritize upgrading Neutralize early. Winning Silent runs consistently utilized Neutralize+ to reliably apply Weak and mitigate incoming damage across long combats, whereas the majority of losing runs left it unupgraded.
After an 8+ turn Act 1 fight, treat the next card reward as a damage check and add frontload, scaling, or draw instead of more passive defense.
On Silent, if you reach late Act 2 or Act 3 still carrying roughly 8+ starter Strikes/Defends, treat that as a build failure: prioritize removes and skip filler until basics stop being core plays.

By late Act 1, do not remain starter-dense. The winning decks either became very lean or were filled out with real engines/support cards, while the losses still had Strike and Defend as core cards and among the most-played cards. Prioritize removes and reward picks that displace starters before elite/boss checks.

Shiv Burst decks must be optimized for speed against late-game bosses (Door, Queen), aiming to finish the fight in under 10 rounds. Without a dedicated block engine (like Blur+), Shiv decks that allow boss combats to drag on to 14-18 rounds consistently succumb to lethal attrition.

Incorporate block-producing draw cards (like Backflip or Flick-Flack) to support your shiv generation. Winning runs use these utility cards to cycle and survive, while losing runs suffer lethal damage by over-relying on basic Defends and unupgraded Neutralize for block.

Prioritize upgrading primary Shiv generators (Blade Dance, Cloak and Dagger) at rest sites to accelerate damage output. Winning Shiv runs used upgraded generators to burst down late-game Bosses in 6-8 rounds, minimizing damage taken, whereas losing runs left them unupgraded and suffered fatal attrition in 14-18 round fights.

Do not rely on sheer volume of base-damage Shivs to clear Act 3 bosses. Draft and upgrade secondary scaling damage (Predator+, Noxious Fumes+) to finish boss fights (Queen, Door) in under 12 rounds. Runs that lacked damage-per-shiv scaling stalled into 14-18 round combats and ultimately lost despite playing over 150-250 Shivs.

Shiv decks must push for fast lethality against scaling bosses like the Queen and the Door (aiming for under 13 rounds). Winning runs closed out these boss fights in 6 to 13 rounds, while runs that lacked the burst to finish quickly were dragged out to 14-18 rounds and lost to boss scaling.

Prioritize upgrading primary Shiv generators (like Blade Dance or Cloak and Dagger) or securing alternate scaling (such as Noxious Fumes) for the mid-to-late game. Winning decks consistently feature upgraded Shiv sources or Poison to maintain damage output, whereas decks relying on unupgraded generators fail to scale and die in prolonged late-game encounters.

Late-game bosses (The Door, Queen+Torch Head Amalgam) are strict damage races. Winning runs defeated these bosses in 6 to 10 rounds, whereas runs that dragged the fight out to 14-18 rounds consistently lost. Draft strong upfront burst or secondary scaling (like Noxious Fumes+) to ensure you can end Act 3 boss fights before their scaling damage overwhelms your block.

Prioritize upgrading core Shiv generators (Blade Dance, Cloak and Dagger) at rest sites when playing Shiv-focused archetypes. Winning runs consistently relied on upgraded versions to reach critical damage scaling, whereas losing runs stalled and died with unupgraded copies.

Extremely long combats (20+ rounds) severely drain HP and indicate a fatal lack of scaling damage. Avoid relying solely on 'block stall' or 'weak stall' archetypes without drafting a clear scaling win condition (e.g., poison engines, shiv payoffs).

Do not rely on pure block or weak stall archetypes. Lacking scaling damage leads to severely protracted combats (25+ rounds) that result in guaranteed attrition and death by Act 2 (e.g., floor 17). Draft scaling damage like Noxious Fumes or synergized Shivs to keep combat lengths under 15 rounds.

Avoid keeping the deck extremely thin (under 15 cards) in Act 1 if you lack strong damage scaling. Runs that entered Floor 17 (Act 1 Boss) with 12-14 cards relying entirely on flat damage attacks and basic Defends died after agonizingly long 26-32 round combats because they could not outpace boss scaling.

Avoid aggressively skipping card rewards to thin your deck in Act 1. Arriving at the Act 1 Boss with only 12-14 cards and a high density of basic Strikes and Defends lacks the damage output required to survive, leading to lethal 25+ round combats by attrition.

Do not aggressively thin your deck in Act 1 without a strong damage scaling engine. Losing runs that heavily thinned their decks (12-14 cards) without scaling damage inevitably stalled out, dying in fatal 26-32 round encounters against Floor 17 bosses. Winning decks naturally hovered around 20-26 cards by taking impactful additions.

Draft scaling damage (such as Poison, Phantom Blades, or Leading Strike) before facing the Act 1 Boss. Relying solely on basic block stalling and flat damage (Strikes, Dagger Spray) against bosses like Lagavulin Matriarch and Waterfall Giant will force unsurvivable 25+ round combats.

Prioritize dedicated damage scaling to avoid fatal late-Act-1 stalls. Losing runs repeatedly died in grueling 26-32 round combats around floor 17 (e.g., vs Lagavulin Matriarch or Waterfall Giant), indicating that relying purely on basic block and unscaled strikes leads to insurmountable attrition.

Secure immediate damage scaling or burst before Act 1/Act 2 elites and bosses to prevent fatal attrition. Losing runs that relied too heavily on basic Strike/Defend patterns without scaling damage stalled out, resulting in lethal 30+ round combats against sturdy enemies like Lagavulin Matriarch and Waterfall Giant.

Draft and play frontloaded damage before taking on early scaling Elites. Over-relying on defensive setups (Defend, Deflect, Survivor) without adequate damage tools leads to fatal attrition wars, as evidenced by catastrophic 30-round losses to the Lagavulin Matriarch.

Prepare for extreme attrition against late-game multi-enemy encounters like Queen + Torch Head Amalgam or Test Subject. These fights frequently stretch to 14-24 rounds; unless you have a highly accelerated damage engine (winning in <10 rounds), entering with low to middling HP (e.g., 45 HP) risks fatal chip damage, requiring a healthy HP pool and dense mitigation to outlast.

Prioritize upgrading Neutralize early. Across both physical and shiv successful runs, Neutralize+ sees exceptionally high play rates. Repeatedly applying Weakness is critical for mitigating damage in prolonged multi-enemy or boss fights (like Queen + Torch Head Amalgam), especially when paired with 0-cost cycle tools like Escape Plan.

When at late-act rest sites or routing map choices before bosses like Test Subject or the Queen, heavily prioritize resting/healing if your health is below 45. Winning Silent runs routinely absorb 30-40 unavoidable damage over these 10-20 round endurance fights, whereas runs entering these boss encounters with 30-35 HP consistently face early lethal before their deck can scale.

Preserve HP aggressively in the late Acts to ensure you enter the final boss fights with a substantial health buffer (50+ HP). Winning runs entered Test Subject and Queen fights with high HP and successfully endured 10-20 rounds of attrition, whereas runs entering with critical/low HP (31-45 HP) lacked the buffer to survive the initial setup phase and were killed in 6-7 rounds.

Rest before late Act 3 bosses (Queen, Test Subject) to ensure you enter with at least 50 HP. Winning runs preserved HP efficiently and entered these F48 fights with 49-80 HP, whereas runs that entered with ~30 HP consistently lost despite having similar viable archetypes.

Prioritize upgrading Neutralize early in the run. Nearly all successful runs and deep runs (reaching Act 3+) rely heavily on playing Neutralize+ for zero-cost damage mitigation, whereas runs that fail in Act 1 or early Act 2 typically leave it unupgraded.

Commit entirely to a single damage scaling archetype (either Shivs or Poison). Losing runs frequently attempted to hybridize poison and shivs, leading to a lack of burst or scaling, whereas winning runs strictly specialized in one engine (e.g., Shiv Spam, Poison Stall).

Prioritize upgrading Neutralize early. Winning runs consistently had Neutralize+ among their top 5 most played cards to reliably apply Weak and mitigate damage, whereas losing runs often ignored it in favor of upgrading basic attacks like Poisoned Stab or Ultimate Strike.

Commit clearly to either a Shiv-based scaling engine or a Poison-based stall engine. Avoid drafting hybrid 'shiv and poison' decks, as splitting your synergies leads to inadequate scaling in later acts.
Upgrade Neutralize early or aggressively draft persistent damage mitigation (like Suppress or Escape Plan) to minimize HP bleed during long boss and elite encounters.

Ensure high defensive density in Shiv-based decks. Winners consistently played 'Defend' or block cards 70+ times across the run, even in offensive archetypes. Losses occurred when Shiv volume was high but block plays remained under 40, leading to attrition in long combats (10+ rounds).

Prioritize entering the Test Subject boss fight with high HP (60+) and prepare for a long attrition battle. Winning runs endured 15-20 rounds and absorbed 40-50 cumulative damage. Entering this fight with low HP (around 30) leads to rapid losses, as burst damage alone is insufficient.

Do not hybridize Shiv and Poison archetypes. Runs that mixed Poison cards (Deadly Poison, Noxious Fumes) into Shiv decks suffered from split synergies and poor HP efficiency in late-game hallway fights, entering late Act bosses with critical HP (e.g., 31 HP vs Test Subject) and failing. Commit completely to pure Shiv scaling or pure Poison.

Support Shiv generation with strong card draw and efficient block engines. Cards like Backflip, Escape Plan, and Dodge and Roll are crucial for sustaining the 15-20 round boss encounters typically required for a Shiv scaling deck to win.

In Shiv-centric decks, prioritize defensive scaling and draw (Backflip, Dodge and Roll, Escape Plan) to survive long-duration boss encounters. Winning runs against 'Test Subject' and 'Door' lasted 15-20 rounds, whereas losing runs were eliminated within 7-8 rounds.

In Shiv archetypes, prioritize 'scaling' and sustained defense (like Backflip, Escape Plan, or Dodge and Roll) over pure 'burst' damage cards (like Assassinate). Winning runs successfully survived late-game bosses (e.g., Test Subject) by sustaining for 15-20 rounds, whereas burst-focused decks lacked the defensive density and died in 7-8 rounds.

In Silent Shiv decks, prioritize drafting 'scaling' or 'spam' engines that include sustained card draw and defense (e.g., Backflip, Escape Plan, Cloak and Dagger) over pure burst damage. Late-game bosses like Test Subject require 15-20 rounds of sustained defense to defeat, which aggressive burst decks fail to achieve, resulting in rapid deaths.

Upgrade early mitigation and shiv utility cards like Neutralize and Cloak and Dagger. Winning runs consistently utilized Neutralize+ and Cloak and Dagger+ to maximize block efficiency per draw, whereas late-game losses (Floors 30-48) struggled with unupgraded versions of these same cards.

If Silent is still starter-dense by late Act 1, treat that as a warning sign: prioritize removes and only add real archetype payoffs or draw/block support. The losses at floors 8, 17, and 33 were still mostly Strike/Defend decks, while winning runs either trimmed down to 9-13 cards or had a clearly functional engine despite larger size.
Late Act 1, if the deck still has no AoE or multi-target payoff, spend the next card reward on that gap instead of another single-target filler pick.

On Silent, a starter-dense deck by late Act 2 is a major loss signal; prioritize removals and high-impact card rewards over filler. The losing runs still relied heavily on Strike/Defend, while winning runs either thinned down hard or had enough non-starter utility that basic cards stopped being the core plan.

By late Act 1 into Act 2 on Silent, treat a deck as underpowered if Strike/Defend are still your most-played cards; prioritize removes and higher-impact reward picks until your turns are centered on engine cards instead of starters.

If you still have 4+ Strikes by late Act 1, prioritize Strike removes over adding more mediocre attack cards. The losses stayed starter-heavy (often 3-5 Strikes at death), while most wins had already cut down to 0-2 Strikes or fully replaced starter damage with stronger engines.

In late-act Silent shiv decks that are still starter-dense, stop taking more chip/shiv cards and prioritize block-draw-control tools instead (for example Backflip, Deflect, Acrobatics, Piercing Wail, or Malaise). The winning shiv runs were the HP-efficient ones; the losing shiv runs were mostly Strike/Defend/Shiv/Cloak-and-Dagger shells that ran out of life.
